A  supporting half-space  to a subset  
                         S 
                       of  
                         
                            
                               R 
                            
                            
                               n 
                            
                         
                       is a closed half-space which contains  
                         S 
                       and has a point of  
                         S 
                       in its boundary. A  supporting hyperplane  to  
                         S 
                       is a hyperplane which is the boundary of a supporting half-space to  
                         S 
                      . In other words, a supporting hyperplane to  
                         S 
                       is a hyperplane which can be represented in the form  
                         
                            { 
                            
                               x 
                            
                            : 
                            
                               〈 
                               
                                  x 
                               
                               , 
                               
                               
                                  b 
                               
                               〉 
                            
                            = 
                            β 
                            } 
                         
                      ,  
                         
                            b 
                         
                         ≠ 
                         
                            0 
                         
                      , where  
                         
                            〈 
                            
                               x 
                            
                            , 
                            
                            
                               b 
                            
                            〉 
                         
                         ≤ 
                         β 
                       for every  
                         
                            x 
                         
                         ∈ 
                       conv  
                         S 
                      , and  
                         
                            〈 
                            
                               x 
                            
                            , 
                            
                            
                               b 
                            
                            〉 
                         
                         = 
                         β 
                       for at least one  
                         
                            x 
                         
                         ∈ 
                         
                            conv 
                         
                         
                         S 
                      . Each supporting hyperplane passing through a given point  
                         
                            a 
                         
                         ∈ 
                         
                            conv 
                         
                         
                         S 
                       corresponds to a vector  
                         
                            b 
                         
                       such that  
                         
                            〈 
                            
                               x 
                            
                            − 
                            
                               a 
                            
                            , 
                            
                            
                               b 
                            
                            〉 
                         
                         ≤ 
                         0 
                       for every  
                         
                            x 
                         
                         ∈ 
                         
                            conv 
                         
                         
                         S 
                      , i.e., a vector  
                         
                            b 
                         
                       that does not make an acute angle with any line segment in  
                         S 
                       with  
                         
                            a 
                         
                       as end point. Such  
                         
                            b 
                         
                       is called a  normal  to  
                         S 
                       at  
                         
                            a 
                         
                         ∈ 
                         
                            conv 
                         
                         
                         S 
                      . Thus, a supporting hyperplane to  
                         S 
                       is associated with a linear function which achieves its maximum on conv  
                         S 
                      , i.e.,  
                         
                            〈 
                            
                               x 
                            
                            , 
                            
                            
                               b 
                            
                            〉 
                         
                         ≤ 
                         
                            〈 
                            
                               a 
                            
                            , 
                            
                            
                               b 
                            
                            〉 
                         
                       for every  
                         
                            x 
                         
                         ∈ 
                         
                            conv 
                         
                         
                         S 
                      . It can be proved that every supporting hyperplane to  
                         S 
                       is a supporting hyperplane to conv  
                         S 
                       and every supporting hyperplane to conv  
                         S 
                       is a supporting hyperplane to  
                         S 
                      .

Our proposed characterization of geodesic arcs in the small is based on the concept of face of a convex set ( Rockafellar, 1970 , p. 162).  
                         Definition 4 
                         A  face  of a convex set  
                               C 
                             is a convex subset  
                               K 
                             of  
                               C 
                             such that every closed line segment in  
                               C 
                             with a relative interior point in  
                               K 
                             has both endpoints in  
                               K 
                            .

A  face  of a convex set  
                               C 
                             is a convex subset  
                               K 
                             of  
                               C 
                             such that every closed line segment in  
                               C 
                             with a relative interior point in  
                               K 
                             has both endpoints in  
                               K 
                            .

The intersection of  
                         C 
                       with any of its supporting hyperplanes is a face of  
                         C 
                      . A point  
                         
                            v 
                         
                         ∈ 
                         C 
                       is an  extreme point  of  
                         C 
                       if  
                         
                            v 
                         
                       cannot be represented as a strict convex combination of two distinct points in  
                         C 
                      . In other words, if  
                         
                            v 
                         
                         = 
                         λ 
                         
                            y 
                         
                         + 
                         
                            ( 
                            1 
                            − 
                            λ 
                            ) 
                         
                         
                            z 
                         
                       with  
                         λ 
                         ∈ 
                         
                            ( 
                            0 
                            , 
                            1 
                            ) 
                         
                       and  
                         
                            y 
                         
                         , 
                         
                         
                            z 
                         
                         ∈ 
                         C 
                      , then  
                         
                            v 
                         
                         = 
                         
                            y 
                         
                         = 
                         
                            z 
                         
                      . The extreme points of  
                         C 
                       are zero-dimensional faces of  
                         C 
                      , forming a set denoted by  
                         E 
                         
                            ( 
                            C 
                            ) 
                         
                      .

The following example serves to illustrate the concept of face and to point out that not all closed line segments in a convex set are faces of that set. Let  
                         C 
                       be the convex set whose boundary is formed by the following four closed line segments: from (1,0) to (0,1), from (0,1) to  
                         
                            ( 
                            − 
                            1 
                            , 
                            0 
                            ) 
                         
                      , from  
                         
                            ( 
                            − 
                            1 
                            , 
                            0 
                            ) 
                         
                       to  
                         
                            ( 
                            0 
                            , 
                            − 
                            1 
                            ) 
                         
                       and from  
                         
                            ( 
                            0 
                            , 
                            − 
                            1 
                            ) 
                         
                       to (1,0), respectively, see  Fig. 1 
                      . These four closed line segments are  the  faces of  
                         C 
                      . Any other line segment in  
                         C 
                       is not a face of  
                         C 
                      . For example, consider the closed line segment  
                         K 
                       joining  
                         
                            ( 
                            − 
                            1 
                            , 
                            0 
                            ) 
                         
                       and (1,0) in  
                         C 
                      , and let  
                         L 
                       be the closed line segment joining  
                         
                            ( 
                            0 
                            , 
                            − 
                            
                               
                                  1 
                               
                               
                                  2 
                               
                            
                            ) 
                         
                       and  
                         
                            ( 
                            0 
                            , 
                            
                               
                                  1 
                               
                               
                                  2 
                               
                            
                            ) 
                         
                       in  
                         C 
                      . The point (0,0) is an interior point of  
                         L 
                       in  
                         K 
                      , but the endpoints  
                         
                            ( 
                            0 
                            , 
                            
                            
                               
                                  1 
                               
                               
                                  2 
                               
                            
                            ) 
                         
                       and  
                         
                            ( 
                            0 
                            , 
                            − 
                            
                               
                                  1 
                               
                               
                                  2 
                               
                            
                            ) 
                         
                       are not in  
                         K 
                      . Thus the closed line segment  
                         K 
                       joining  
                         
                            ( 
                            − 
                            1 
                            , 
                            0 
                            ) 
                         
                       and (1,0) is not a face of  
                         C 
                      .  
                         Lemma 1 
                         
                            If  
                            
                               K 
                             
                            and  
                            
                               J 
                             
                            are faces of a convex set  
                            
                               C 
                             
                            such that  
                            
                               
                                  ri 
                               
                               
                               K 
                               ∩ 
                               
                                  ri 
                               
                               
                               J 
                               ≠ 
                               ∅ 
                            
                            , then  
                            
                               K 
                               = 
                               J 
                            
                            .

If  
                            
                               K 
                             
                            and  
                            
                               J 
                             
                            are faces of a convex set  
                            
                               C 
                             
                            such that  
                            
                               
                                  ri 
                               
                               
                               K 
                               ∩ 
                               
                                  ri 
                               
                               
                               J 
                               ≠ 
                               ∅ 
                            
                            , then  
                            
                               K 
                               = 
                               J 
                            
                            .

Proof 
                         
                            Rockafellar, 1970 , p. 164 
                         Let  
                               
                                  z 
                               
                               ∈ 
                               
                                  ri 
                               
                               
                               K 
                               ∩ 
                               
                                  ri 
                               
                               
                               J 
                            . If  
                               
                                  x 
                               
                             is any point of  
                               J 
                             other than  
                               
                                  z 
                               
                            , there exists a  
                               
                                  y 
                               
                               ∈ 
                               J 
                             such that  
                               
                                  z 
                               
                             is in the relative interior of the line segment joining  
                               
                                  x 
                               
                             and  
                               
                                  y 
                               
                            . Since  
                               K 
                             is a face,  
                               
                                  x 
                               
                             and  
                               
                                  y 
                               
                             must be in  
                               K 
                            . Thus  
                               J 
                               ⊆ 
                               K 
                            . Similarly,  
                               K 
                               ⊆ 
                               J 
                            , and then  
                               K 
                               = 
                               J 
                            . □ 
                       We need the following statement, which is proved in  Rockafellar (1970, p. 166) :  
                         Lemma 2 
                         
                            Any closed bounded convex set  
                            
                               C 
                             
                            is the convex closure of its extreme points,  
                            
                               C 
                               = 
                               
                                  conv 
                               
                               
                               E 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  C 
                                  ) 
                               
                            
                            .

Let  
                               
                                  z 
                               
                               ∈ 
                               
                                  ri 
                               
                               
                               K 
                               ∩ 
                               
                                  ri 
                               
                               
                               J 
                            . If  
                               
                                  x 
                               
                             is any point of  
                               J 
                             other than  
                               
                                  z 
                               
                            , there exists a  
                               
                                  y 
                               
                               ∈ 
                               J 
                             such that  
                               
                                  z 
                               
                             is in the relative interior of the line segment joining  
                               
                                  x 
                               
                             and  
                               
                                  y 
                               
                            . Since  
                               K 
                             is a face,  
                               
                                  x 
                               
                             and  
                               
                                  y 
                               
                             must be in  
                               K 
                            . Thus  
                               J 
                               ⊆ 
                               K 
                            . Similarly,  
                               K 
                               ⊆ 
                               J 
                            , and then  
                               K 
                               = 
                               J 
                            . □

The boundary of conv

                            F 
                            )

                            )

                       hits

                       is denoted by

                            ( 
                            
                               v

                            Busemann and Mayer (1941, Theorem 2, p. 184)  proved that all the directions

                            , with

In this section, we briefly discuss basic concepts about the proposed characterization of Fechnerian geodesic arcs in the small, and then we describe how this characterization could be used to find the shape of the Fechnerian indicatrices.                             x

                            x 
                         
                      , which “has a practical significance for computing Fechnerian distances, especially in the case of “brute force” techniques utilizing fine-mesh discretizations of a stimulus space” ( Dzhafarov & Colonius, 2001 , p. 695).

Finally, we want to note that the following statements are equivalent: (a)  
                         F 
                       is convex; (b) for each tangent vector  
                         
                            u 
                         
                      ,

                            } 
                         
                       is a

                      -minimizing set; (c)  
                         F 
                         = 
                         
                            
                               F 
                            
                            
                               ∗ 
                            
                         
                      ; (d) the distance measure in the small  
                         d 
                       fulfills the triangle inequality, and (e) the Fechnerian distance  
                         G 
                       is equal to the distance measure in the small  
                         d 
                       ( Sánchez-Larios & Guillén-Burguete, 2010 ).
